lessons.title: Learn: Mastering the Core Shell
lessons.content_md:
# Lab 01: Mastering the Core Shell

> Read every section carefully before moving to practice. This knowledge is the foundation for all future labs.

---

## 1. What is Linux?

Linux is a free, open-source operating system kernel created by Linus Torvalds in 1991. It powers:

- 96% of the world's top web servers
- All major cloud platforms (AWS, GCP, Azure)
- Android smartphones
- Network routers, IoT devices, supercomputers
- Cybersecurity tools and penetration testing environments

Linux is not just an OS вҖ” it is the language of servers and security professionals.

---

## 2. The Shell and Terminal

When you open a terminal, you are talking to the **shell**.

| Term | What it means |
|---|---|
| **Kernel** | Core of Linux вҖ” manages CPU, memory, hardware |
| **Shell** | Program that reads your commands and runs them |
| **Terminal** | Window/interface to access the shell |
| **bash** | Default shell on most Linux systems (Bourne Again SHell) |

### The Command Prompt

```
hacker@bhackme:~$
  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ $ means regular user (# means root)
  в”Ӯ        в”Ӯ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ ~ means you are in your home directory
  в”Ӯ        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ hostname of the machine
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ your username
```

### Command Syntax Rule

```
command  [OPTIONS]  [ARGUMENTS]
   в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ
   в”Ӯ          в”Ӯ          в””в”Җв”Җ what to act on (file, path, text)
   в”Ӯ          в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ flags that change behaviour (-a, -l, -r)
   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ the program to run
```

### Keyboard Shortcuts You Must Know

| Shortcut | Action |
|---|---|
| `Tab` | Autocomplete file/command name |
| `Ctrl + C` | Cancel running command |
| `Ctrl + L` | Clear the terminal screen |
| `вҶ‘` / `вҶ“` | Scroll through command history |
| `Ctrl + A` | Jump to start of line |
| `Ctrl + E` | Jump to end of line |

---

## 3. Linux File System Hierarchy

Linux organises everything as files inside one tree starting from `/` (root).

```
/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ bin/       Core system commands (ls, cp, mv, rm)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ etc/       System config files (hostname, passwd, ssh)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ home/      User home directories
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ hacker/  Your personal workspace
в”ңв”Җв”Җ root/      Home directory for the root (admin) user
в”ңв”Җв”Җ var/       Logs, databases, mail, cache
в”ңв”Җв”Җ tmp/       Temporary files (cleared on reboot)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ usr/       User-installed programs and libraries
в”ңв”Җв”Җ proc/      Virtual files showing live kernel/process info
в””в”Җв”Җ dev/       Device files (disks, terminals, null)
```

### Paths

| Type | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute** | `/home/hacker/lab1` | Full path from root `/` |
| **Relative** | `lab1/notes.txt` | Path from your current location |
| `~` | `~/lab1` | Shortcut for your home directory |
| `..` | `cd ..` | Go up one directory |
| `.` | `./script.sh` | Current directory |

---

## 4. Navigation Commands

These three commands are used in every session вҖ” memorise them.

| Command | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `pwd` | Print Working Directory вҖ” shows where you are | `pwd` |
| `ls` | List files and folders | `ls /etc` |
| `ls -la` | List all files including hidden, with full details | `ls -la ~` |
| `cd path` | Change Directory | `cd /etc` |
| `cd ~` | Go to home directory | `cd ~` |
| `cd ..` | Go up one level | `cd ..` |

### Reading `ls -la` Output

```
-rw-r--r-- 1 hacker hacker 124 Apr 2 10:00 notes.txt
в”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в”Ӯ               в””в”Җв”Җ filename
в”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ       в”Ӯ      в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ file size in bytes
в”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯ    в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ owner group
в”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ owner user
в”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯ
в”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв””в””в””в”Җв”Җ others: r=read, -=no write, -=no execute
в”Ӯв”Ӯв”Ӯв””в””в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ group:  r=read, -=no write, -=no execute
в””в””в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ owner:  r=read, w=write,  -=no execute
в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җ file type: - = file, d = directory, l = symbolic link
```

---

## 5. File and Directory Operations

### Creating

| Command | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `mkdir name` | Create a directory | `mkdir ~/lab1` |
| `mkdir -p a/b/c` | Create nested directories | `mkdir -p ~/lab1/docs/notes` |
| `touch file.txt` | Create an empty file | `touch ~/lab1/notes.txt` |
| `echo 'text' > file` | Create file with content | `echo 'hello' > file.txt` |

### Copying and Moving

| Command | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `cp src dest` | Copy file to new location | `cp notes.txt backup.txt` |
| `cp -r dir/ dest/` | Copy entire directory | `cp -r lab1/ lab1_bk/` |
| `mv src dest` | Move or rename file/directory | `mv backup.txt archive.txt` |

### Removing

| Command | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `rm file` | Delete a file | `rm archive.txt` |
| `rm -r dir/` | Delete directory and contents | `rm -r ~/lab1/temp/` |

> **DANGER** вҖ” Never run `rm -rf /` or `rm -rf ~`. These delete everything permanently.

---

## 6. Viewing File Content

| Command | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `cat file` | Print entire file | `cat notes.txt` |
| `head -n 5 file` | Show first 5 lines | `head -n 5 /etc/passwd` |
| `tail -n 5 file` | Show last 5 lines | `tail -n 5 /var/log/syslog` |
| `wc -l file` | Count lines in file | `wc -l notes.txt` |
| `wc -w file` | Count words in file | `wc -w notes.txt` |

---

## 7. Searching

### grep вҖ” Search text inside files

```bash
grep 'pattern' filename         # basic search
grep -i 'pattern' filename      # case-insensitive
grep -r 'pattern' directory/    # search in all files recursively
grep -n 'pattern' filename      # show line numbers
```

Example:
```bash
grep 'Linux' ~/lab1/notes.txt
grep -r 'hacker' /etc/
```

### find вҖ” Locate files by name or type

```bash
find /path -name 'filename'     # find by exact name
find /path -name '*.txt'        # find by pattern
find /path -type d              # find directories only
find /path -type f              # find files only
```

Example:
```bash
find ~/lab1 -name 'notes.txt'
find /etc -name 'passwd'
```

---

## 8. File Permissions

Every file in Linux has three permission groups: owner, group, and others.

### Permission Bits

```
r  w  x     r  -  -     r  -  -
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җ execute for others
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ write for others
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ read for others
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ execute for group
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ write for group
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ read for group
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ execute for owner
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ write for owner
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ read for owner
```

### chmod Numeric Mode

Each permission has a numeric value: read=4, write=2, execute=1.

| Mode | Binary | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `400` | r-------- | Owner read only |
| `600` | rw------- | Owner read and write |
| `700` | rwx------ | Owner full access |
| `644` | rw-r--r-- | Owner rw, others read only |
| `755` | rwxr-xr-x | Owner full, others read/execute |

```bash
chmod 600 notes.txt      # secure private file
chmod 700 scripts/       # private executable directory
chmod 755 public.sh      # script everyone can run
```

> **Security rule** вҖ” sensitive files like SSH keys and password files should always be `600` or `400`. Never `777`.

---

## 9. System Information Commands

| Command | What it shows | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `whoami` | Your current username | `whoami` |
| `id` | Your UID, GID, and groups | `id` |
| `uname -a` | Full kernel and OS version | `uname -a` |
| `hostname` | Machine hostname | `hostname` |
| `history` | Your recent command history | `history | tail -n 20` |
| `date` | Current date and time | `date` |

---

## 10. Pipes and Redirection

Pipes and redirects are what make the shell powerful.

| Operator | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `\|` | Pipe вҖ” send output of one command to another | `cat file \| wc -l` |
| `>` | Redirect output to file (overwrite) | `echo 'hi' > file.txt` |
| `>>` | Append output to file | `echo 'hi' >> file.txt` |
| `<` | Read input from file | `wc -l < file.txt` |

```bash
ls -la /etc | grep passwd      # filter output
cat /etc/passwd | wc -l        # count lines
uname -a | grep Linux           # extract match
```

---

## 11. Safety Rules for This Lab

1. Always run `pwd` before destructive commands so you know where you are.
2. Never use `rm -rf` without double-checking the path argument.
3. Use `Tab` to autocomplete paths вҖ” prevents typos.
4. Use `history` to review what you ran if something goes wrong.
5. Use `Ctrl + C` to stop a command that is running or stuck.
6. Permissions `777` on any file is a security risk вҖ” avoid it.

---

## Ready?

You have now read all the theory for this lab. Move on to the **Command Examples** section to see every command in action, then complete the **Exercises** and the **MCQ Quiz** to earn your points.

lessons.title: Command Examples Part 1 (10 Commands)
lessons.content_md:
## Guided Commands: Part 1

Run each command in order. These steps prepare your workspace for the rest of the lab.

```bash
pwd
whoami
ls /
ls -la ~
cd /etc && pwd
cd ~ && pwd
mkdir -p ~/lab1 && ls ~
touch ~/lab1/notes.txt && ls ~/lab1
echo 'Linux beginner lab' > ~/lab1/notes.txt && cat ~/lab1/notes.txt
cat ~/lab1/notes.txt | wc -w
```

tasks.description: C01: Print your current directory.
C02: Show your current username.
C03: List folders from root and verify /etc appears.
C04: Show hidden files in home with detailed output.
C05: Move to /etc and print path.
C06: Return to home and print path.
C07: Create directory lab1 in home and verify it exists.
C08: Create notes.txt inside lab1 and list the folder.
C09: Write text to notes.txt and print file content.
C10: Count words in notes.txt.

lessons.title: Command Examples Part 2 (10 Commands)
lessons.content_md:
## Guided Commands: Part 2

Continue with the next 10 commands.

```bash
cp ~/lab1/notes.txt ~/lab1/notes_backup.txt && ls ~/lab1
mv ~/lab1/notes_backup.txt ~/lab1/archive.txt && ls ~/lab1
head -n 1 ~/lab1/notes.txt
tail -n 1 ~/lab1/notes.txt
grep 'Linux' ~/lab1/notes.txt
chmod 600 ~/lab1/notes.txt && ls -l ~/lab1/notes.txt
history | tail -n 5
find ~/lab1 -name 'notes.txt'
uname -a | grep Linux
rm ~/lab1/archive.txt && echo removed
```

tasks.description: C11: Copy notes.txt to notes_backup.txt.
C12: Rename notes_backup.txt to archive.txt.
C13: Show the first line of notes.txt.
C14: Show the last line of notes.txt.
C15: Search for Linux in notes.txt.
C16: Set notes.txt permission to 600 and verify.
C17: Display your recent command history.
C18: Find notes.txt inside lab1.
C19: Print Linux system info and filter Linux text.
C20: Remove archive.txt and print confirmation.

lessons.title: Exercises (5)
lessons.content_md:
## Practical Exercises

Complete these five hands-on exercises.

- E1 and E2 are easy
- E3 and E4 are medium
- E5 is medium-hard

Use the command examples from the previous lessons.

tasks.description: E1: Create ~/lab1/docs and ~/lab1/scripts, then list ~/lab1.
E2: Create two lines in ~/lab1/docs/todo.txt, then show total line count.
E3: Copy todo.txt to scripts as todo_backup.txt and list scripts folder.
E4: Find todo_backup.txt from home directory.
E5: Set permission 700 on ~/lab1/scripts and verify with ls -ld.

lessons.title: MCQ Quiz (5)
lessons.content_md:
## MCQ: Linux Fundamentals

Answer using option index (0-3).

Example: If option 2 is correct, submit `2`.

These questions check your understanding of the same topic you practiced.

tasks.description: Q1: Which command prints your current working directory?
Q2: Which command shows hidden files with details?
Q3: Which command is used to search text inside a file?
Q4: What does chmod 600 mean for a file?
tasks.validation_rule: ["Everyone can read/write/execute","Owner can execute only","Group can write","Owner can read and write only"]

tasks.description: Q5: Which command helps locate files by name?
